title: Writing - Part 1: Animal Rights and Scientific Research
description: Your class has attended a debate on the ethical issues surrounding the use of animals in scientific and medical research.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the arguments in your notes. You should explain which argument you consider more persuasive, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Without animal testing, we'd have no vaccines or antibiotics.
Causing suffering to sentient beings for human benefit is unjustifiable.
Lab simulations can now replace most animal experiments entirely.

structure.introduction: Introduce the long-standing ethical controversy surrounding animal experimentation.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Examine the argument that animal testing is a medical necessity that has saved countless human lives.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the moral and scientific case for phasing out animal experiments in favour of alternatives.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both arguments and state which you find more compelling.

content.modelAnswer:
The debate over the use of animals in scientific research is one of the most enduring ethical controversies in modern society, pitting the imperative to advance human medicine against the moral weight we owe to other sentient creatures. This essay will examine the medical necessity argument and the case for transitioning to alternative research methods.

Defenders of animal experimentation argue, with considerable historical justification, that it has been indispensable to medical progress. The development of vaccines, antibiotics, organ transplantation procedures, and treatments for conditions such as diabetes and Parkinson's disease all relied on animal studies at critical stages. Proponents maintain that the biological similarity between many animals and humans makes them irreplaceable as research models for complex systemic conditions, and that strict ethical protocols already exist to minimise suffering.

However, the scientific and moral case for phasing out animal testing has grown significantly stronger in recent decades. Advanced computational models, human-derived organoids, and sophisticated cell culture technologies now offer increasingly accurate simulations of human biological responses—in many cases more accurate than animal models, which frequently fail to predict human outcomes reliably. Furthermore, the moral argument is becoming harder to deflect as neuroscience confirms the capacity of many species for pain, fear, and distress.

In conclusion, I find the case for alternatives more persuasive. While past achievements through animal research are undeniable, the combination of scientific advances and ethical responsibility demands that we commit fully to developing and adopting alternative methods. Continuing to rely on practices we know cause suffering, when credible alternatives exist, is increasingly difficult to defend.
